records.ety: [Cf. OF. escharpe a pilgrim's scrip, or wallet (hanging about the neck), F. charpe sash, scarf; probably from OHG. scharpe pocket; also (from the French) Dan. skiaerf; Sw. skärp, Prov. G. schärfe, LG. scherf, G. schärpe; and also AS. scearf a fragment; possibly akin to E. scrip a wallet. Cf. Scarp a scarf.]

defs: An article of dress of a light and decorative character, worn loosely over the shoulders or about the neck or the waist; a light shawl or handkerchief for the neck; also, a cravat; a neckcloth.
quotes: Put on your hood and scarf.
With care about the banners, scarves, and staves.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Scarfed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Scarfing.]

defs: To throw on loosely; to put on like a scarf.
To dress with a scarf, or as with a scarf; to cover with a loose wrapping.

records.ety: [Sw. skarfva to eke out, to join together, skarf a seam, joint; cf. Dan. skarre to joint, to unite timber, Icel. skara to clinch the planks of a boat, G. scharben to chop, to cut small.]

defs: To form a scarf on the end or edge of, as for a joint in timber, metal rods, etc.
To unite, as two pieces of timber or metal, by a scarf joint.
In a piece which is to be united to another by a scarf joint, the part of the end or edge that is tapered off, rabbeted, or notched so as to be thinner than the rest of the piece.
